coredata.dc:title: A label-free three potential ratiometric electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for cardiac troponin I based on N-(4-aminobutyl)-N-ethylisoluminol functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Mingquan
Shu, Jiangnan
Du, Dexin
Haghighatbin, Mohammad A.
Yang, Di
Bian, Zhiping
Cui, Hua
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we synthesized a novel ECL nanoluminophore N-(4-aminobutyl)-N-ethylisoluminol functionalized graphene quantum dots (ABEI@GQDs), which exhibited three completely potential-resolved dual-color ECL emissions with coreactant K2S2O8 in an aqueous solution. Blue color ECL-1, orange red color ECL-2 and blue color ECL-3 were observed at peak potential of -0.58, -1.85 and 1.20 V, respectively. On this basis, a label-free three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensor was developed for detecting cardiac troponin I (cTnI) by layer-by-layer assembly of ABEI@GQDs and antibody on a chitosan modified fluorine doped tin oxide electrode. In the presence of cTnI, ECL-2 increased, whereas ECL-1 and ECL-3 changed slightly and synchronously. It was found that ECL-1/ECL-3 ratio almost unchanged with increasing the concentration of cTnI, which laid the foundation for self-correction analysis. Accordingly, three potential ratiometric strategy based on the ratio of three ECL peaks was established for the determination of cTnI with a linear dynamic range from 1.0 fg/mL to 5.0 pg/mL and a detection limit of 0.35 fg/mL, which was superior to most reported ECL methods. Moreover, the developed three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensor can effectively eliminate systematic errors caused by environmental changes and thus offers more precise detection results than two potential ratiometric ECL immonosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Potential-resolved multicolor ECL
Immunosensor
cTnI
ABEI functionalized GQDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and detection of label-free ratiometric ECL immunosensor
Assembly strategy for label-free ratiometric ECL immunosensor
Analytical performance of three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensor
Detection of cTnI in human serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: University of Science and Technology of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The support of this research by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFA0201300 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21804124 , 21874122 and 21527807 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2019M662154 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. WK2060190097 ) and the Chinese Academy of Sciences President’s International Fellowship Initiative (Grant No. 2020PB0062 ) are gratefully acknowledged.

Mohammad A. Haghighatbin received his Ph.D. in 2017 from La Trobe University, Melbourne, Australia. He is currently holding the Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) President’s International Fellowship Initiative (PIFI) postdoctoral fellowship at the University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, China. His research interests include electrochemiluminescence of novel inorganic and organic materials and their applications in miniaturized electroanalytical devices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) AFM image of ABEI@GQDs. (B) FT-IR spectra of GQDs (black curve) and ABEI@GQDs (red curve). (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs, ABEI and ABEI@GQDs. (D) XPS spectra of GQDs (black curve) and ABEI@GQDs (red curve) (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) ECL-potential curves of GQDs, a mixed solution of ABEI and GQDs and ABEI@GQDs. (B) Corresponding CVs of GQDs and ABEI@GQDs in 0.2 M BR buffer (pH = 11.0) containing 0.04 M K2S2O8 at air atmosphere. Inset: enlarging CVs from 0.40 to 1.00 V in (B) PMT: −600 V. (C) 3D ECL spectrum of ABEI@GQDs in a 0.2 M BR buffer solution (pH = 11.0) containing 0.04 M K2S2O8 under air atmosphere.
ECL mechanisms of ABEI@GQDs nanoluminophores using K2S2O8 as a coreactant.

(A) ECL stack-plot of proposed immunosensor at different cTnI concentrations. (B) Linear correlation between logarithms of cTnI concentrations and ratio I 2/I 1/I 3. The inset : a comparison of ECL responses for cTnI (a) with different interfering species, including (b) mixture of BSA, HSA, hFABP, Mb, hIgG and cTnI, (c) blank, (d) BSA, (e) hFABP, (f) hIgG, and (g) HSA, (h) Mb. cTnI concentration: 1.0 × 10−13 g/mL, interfering species concentration: 1.0 × 10-12 g/mL, where Δ(I 2/I 1/I 3) was denoted as the difference value of I 2/I 1/I 3 between interfering specie and blank sample. PMT: −600 V.
Schematic illustration for fabrication of proposed label-free ratiometric ECL immunosensor and for detection of cTnI.
Quantitative determination of cTnI in human serum samples a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean value ± SD of three independent experiments, n = 3.

head.ce:title: A label-free three potential ratiometric electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for cardiac troponin I based on N-(4-aminobutyl)-N-ethylisoluminol functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Soft Matter Chemistry, iChEM (Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemistry for Energy Materials), Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui, 230026, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Soft Matter Chemistry
iChEM (Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemistry for Energy Materials)
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Soft Matter Chemistry, iChEM (Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemistry for Energy Materials), Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230026, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Cardiovascular Disease, First Affiliated Hospital of Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 210029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: First Affiliated Hospital of Nanjing Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Cardiovascular Disease, First Affiliated Hospital of Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210029, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Mingquan Guo and Jiangnan Shu contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we synthesized a novel ECL nanoluminophore N-(4-aminobutyl)-N-ethylisoluminol functionalized graphene quantum dots (ABEI@GQDs), which exhibited three completely potential-resolved dual-color ECL emissions with coreactant K2S2O8 in an aqueous solution. Blue color ECL-1, orange red color ECL-2 and blue color ECL-3 were observed at peak potential of -0.58, -1.85 and 1.20 V, respectively. On this basis, a label-free three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensor was developed for detecting cardiac troponin I (cTnI) by layer-by-layer assembly of ABEI@GQDs and antibody on a chitosan modified fluorine doped tin oxide electrode. In the presence of cTnI, ECL-2 increased, whereas ECL-1 and ECL-3 changed slightly and synchronously. It was found that ECL-1/ECL-3 ratio almost unchanged with increasing the concentration of cTnI, which laid the foundation for self-correction analysis. Accordingly, three potential ratiometric strategy based on the ratio of three ECL peaks was established for the determination of cTnI with a linear dynamic range from 1.0 fg/mL to 5.0 pg/mL and a detection limit of 0.35 fg/mL, which was superior to most reported ECL methods. Moreover, the developed three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensor can effectively eliminate systematic errors caused by environmental changes and thus offers more precise detection results than two potential ratiometric ECL immonosensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Potential-resolved multicolor electrochemiluminescence (ECL) nanoluminophore refers to that a nanoluminophore exhibits two or more ECL emissions with different potential ranges and colors in a single potential scanning process. It has attracted more and more attention due to its good application prospects in precision detection with self-correction, simultaneous detection of multiple targets, and so on [1–4]. Up to now, three types of potential-resolved multicolor ECL nanoluminophores have been reported. The first type is the ECL nanoluminophores without functionalization or hybridization. For example, Pengjia Zhang and coworkers reported a dual-peak ECL system of carbon dots in ethanol using tetrabutyl ammonium bromide as a coreactant upon a negative potential scan, which was used to detect iron ions by the internal standard method [5]. The second type is the ECL nanoluminophores based on the hybridization of two nanomaterials. For instance, Zhili Han and coworkers synthesized a kind of nanographene oxide wrapped TiO2 nanoluminophores with two potential-resolved ECL peaks within negative potential, which was used to construct a label-free ratiometric aptasensor for detecting cardiac troponin I (cTnI) [6]. Moreover, Dexin Du and coworkers reported a kind of MOF-5-wrapped CdS quantum dots with two potential-resolved ECL peaks in the presence of K2S2O8. Based on this, a differential ECL immunosensor was constructed for determination of cTnI [7], which can effectively release the additive systematical error so as to improve the analytical performance. The third type is ECL molecule functionalized nanoluminophores. For example, Jue Wang and coworkers prepared a kind of N-(aminobutyl)-N-(ethylisoluminol) (ABEI) functionalized graphitic carbon nitrides, which exhibited two potential-resolved ECL peaks in the presence of K2S2O8 and H2O2. Based on this, a metal ion-mediated potential-resolved ratiometric ECL bioassay was developed for the detection of miR-133a [8]. However, ratiometric ECL protocols based on potential-resolved ECL nanoluminophores are limited to two potential ratiometric strategy, which might not effectively eliminate systematic errors resulted from environmental changes in some cases. Thus, design of new nanoluminophores with multiple potential-resolved ECL peaks or colors and exploration of new ratiometric strategy are very important to further improve the analytical performance.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is a 0 D graphene material, composed of a 1 or 2 layers atomically thin graphitic plane (< 2 nm thick) with lateral dimensions < 10 nm [9–11]. Their fascinating optical properties, including photoluminescence, photoinduced electron transfer, chemiluminescence, ECL [12–14], have been intensively studied. Particularly, great attention has been paid to ECL due to their potential applications in bioassays. For example, Yongqiang Dong and coworkers reported a method to extract GQDs from activated carbon by chemical oxidation, which exhibited strong ECL activity [15]. Moreover, it is desirable to harness these unique properties of GQDs in nanohybrids through the incorporation of various kinds of functional molecules or materials. The chemical functionalization of GQDs can not only improve material inherent properties, but also can incorporate new properties to GQDs nanohybrids [16,17]. For example, Pu Zhang and coworkers used aminated 3, 4, 9, 10-perylenetetracarboxylic acid to immobilize GQDs and Au@Fe3O4 for the construction of ECL biosensor to detect miRNA-155. The as-prepared GQDs nanohybrids improved water solubility of GQDs and exhibited good film-forming property, which could ensure the stability of the biosensor [18]. Ruizhong Zhang and coworkers prepared a kind of nitrogen- and sulfur-doped GQDs, which could capture more holes from sulfate radical anion (SO4
−) and exhibited high ECL efficiency [19]. However, up to now, the studies about functional GQDs in the ECL filed were mainly focused on the immobilization of GQDs on nanomaterials or polymers for sensing platform and functionalization of GQDs with surface groups to improve their ECL activity. The functionalization of GQDs with ECL molecules or materials have rarely been reported, which is promising to obtain potential-resolved multicolor ECL nanoluminophores.

Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) is one of the most serious diseases affecting humans [20,21]. Thus, rapid and accurate early diagnosis is essential for the treatment of AMI [22,23]. As so far, some methods have been developed to detect cTnI, the gold standard biomarker of AMI [24,25]. However, these methods still have some issues need to be considered, such as sensitivity, accuracy, multistep processing of samples, and high cost. Therefore, a fast, sensitive and accurate cTnI detection method is urgently needed, which is of great significance to reduce AMI mortality.

Herein, a novel ABEI functionalized GQDs (ABEI@GQDs) ECL nanoluminophores with three completely potential-resolved dual-color ECL emissions was successfully synthesized. Atomic force microscopy (AFM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), UV–vis spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used for characterization analysis of the as-prepared ABEI@GQDs. The ECL property of ABEI@GQDs was investigated under cyclic voltammetry condition. It was found that ABEI@GQDs exhibited three potential-resolved ECL peaks at -0.58, -1.85 and 1.20 V in a cyclic voltammetry scan by using K2S2O8 as a coreactant. The ECL mechanisms of the three ECL peaks were explored. Based on this, a label-free ECL immunosensor was designed to detect AMI biomarker cTnI by virtue of assembly of ABEI@GQDs on the surface of a fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) electrode via chitosan, followed by the connection of antibody via amide reaction. After capturing cTnI, ECL-2 increased with the increase of cTnI concentration, whereas ECL-1 and ECL-3 changed slightly and synchronously. It was found that ECL-1/ECL-3 ratio almost unchanged with increasing the concentration of cTnI. Furthermore, it was observed that the intensity ratio of the three ECL peaks increased linearly with increasing cTnI concentration in certain range. Accordingly, a label-free three potential ratiometric immunosensor using ECL-1/ECL-3 ratio as a self-correction reference was established for the detection of cTnI. The analytical conditions were optimized and the analytical performance of the label-free three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensor was examined. The proposed label-free three potential ratiometric ECL strategy was also compared with two potential ratiometric ECL strategy. The practicability of the ECL immunosensor was also investigated by detecting cTnI in human serum samples.

GQDs were synthesized according to the methods as described in previous reports [15] and summarized in the Supporting Information (SI), section S1.2. Subsequently, ABEI@GQDs nanoluminophores were synthesized by the amide coupling reaction between -NH2 group in ABEI and −COOH group in GQDs. Specifically, 2 mL solution containing 10 mg/mL N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-(3-(dimethylamino) propyl)-3-ethyl-carbodiimidehydro-chloride (EDC) was added to 20 mL GQDs suspension, which reacted under magnetic stirring for 30 min. to activate the superficial −COOH group of GQDs. In the following step, a 2 mL ABEI stock solution was added to the above suspension and reacted at 4 °C for 12 h. Finally, the unreacted ABEl was removed by dialysis using a dialysis bag with 1 KD molecular weight in Milli-Q water for 48 h to obtain the purified ABEl@GQDs nanoluminophores.

ABEI@GQDs nanoluminophores were characterized by AFM (Multi-Mode V, Veeco, USA), FT-IR (a Bruker Vector-22 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Instruments, Billerica, MA)), UV–vis spectroscopy (Agilent 8453 UV–vis spectrophotometer, USA), and XPS (an ESCALABMK II electron spectrograph (VG Scientific, UK) with Al Ka radiation as the X-ray source).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and detection of label-free ratiometric ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: 5 μL of 0.1% (w/w) chitosan solution was spotted on a previously rinsed and dried FTO electrode and dried at 26 °C to form the positively-charged polymeric membrane as the substrate. In the following step, the mixture of 25 μL ABEI@GQDs and 5 μL solution containing 10 mg/mL NHS and EDC was dropped on the chitosan membrane and incubated at 26 °C for 1 h, followed by a washing step with 0.01 M PBS. Next, the mixture of 25 μL antibody of cardiac troponin I (anti-cTnI) and 5 μL solution containing 10 mg/mL NHS and EDC was dropped on the electrode surface and incubated at 26 °C for 1 h, followed by a thorough rinse with 0.01 M PBS. Subsequently, 30 μL 1% (w/w) BSA in 0.01 M PBS was spotted on the electrodes for 40 min at 37 °C to block the nonspecific binding sites. Finally, the electrode was thoroughly washed with 0.01 M PBS to form the label-free ratiometric ECL immunosensor. The detection for cTnI is described in SI, section S1.5.

ABEI@GQDs nanoluminophores were synthesized in the following two steps. Initially, GQDs were peeled from activated carbon in a strong acidic HNO3 solution. In virtue of the strong oxidation of HNO3, the as-synthesized GQDs abundantly possessed −COOH functional groups, which were further bound to -NH2 groups of ABEI to obtain ABEI@GQDs nanoluminophores via amide coupling reactions. As shown in Figure S1B, the stability testing of ABEI@GQDs in a month show that there is no significant difference between the ECL intensities. The synthesized nanoluminophores exhibited good stability in a month.

The morphologies of the prepared GQDs and ABEI@GQDs were primarily characterized by AFM. As it is shown in Fig. 1 A, the topographic heights of ABEI@GQDs were in the range of 0.7–2.1 nm with an average height of ∼1.5 nm, comparable to that of GQDs (shown in Figure S1). The result suggested that ABEI@GQDs nanoluminophores possess 1–3 layers graphene nanosheets with sizes and morphologies similar to their parent GQDs. Fig. 1B shows the FT-IR spectra of GQDs and ABEI@GQDs. As it is shown, GQDs exhibited a broad absorption peak at 3430 cm−1 assigned to −OH groups, a shoulder absorption peak of CO group at 1735 cm−1, and two characteristic peaks at 1600 and 1430 cm−1 corresponding to −COO− functional groups, respectively (Fig. 1B, black curve). The results clarified that the graphitic carbon surface of the particles was decorated with abundant −COOH groups, facilitating the further ABEI attachments via amide coupling reaction. Arguably, the FT-IR spectrum of ABEI@GQDs (Fig. 1B, red curve) showed a shift of the CO band to a lower frequency of 1720 cm−1, confirming the formation of the amide bonds in the presence of ABEI. Moreover, the new bands at 1559 and 1252 cm−1 assigned to in-plane N−H and C−N bonds, respectively, also confirmed the successful formation of the amide linkages between the ABEI and GQDs. For the UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 1C, black curve), a typical absorption peak at 230 nm was observed. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of ABEI@GQDs (Fig. 1C, red curve), a characteristic absorption peak of ABEI around 320 nm was observed. The result indicated that ABEI was successfully bound on the surface of GQDs. Moreover, the XPS results (Fig. 1D) indicated that both GQDs and ABEI@GQDs were comprised of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen elements. The traces of nitrogen element in GQDs elemental analysis most likely originated from the decomposition of HNO3 during the immersion of the carbon sheets in the acidic solution, which induced the doping of elemental nitrogen into GQDs surface structure. The elemental analysis results obtained from ABEI@GQDs demonstrated much higher nitrogen contents in comparison to that of GQDs, which further confirmed the presence of ABEI and successful synthesis of ABEI@GQDs.

The ECL properties of ABEI@GQDs nanoluminophores were studied in 0.2 M BR buffer solutions (pH = 11.0) containing 0.04 M K2S2O8. Fig. 2 A shows the ECL signal collected between −2.30 and 1.50 V with a scan rate of 0.05 V/s during cyclic voltammetry at an air atmosphere. It could be seen that three distinct ECL peaks appeared at different potential ranges. During the cathodic sweep of potentials, two intense ECL emissions, ECL-1 and ECL-2, were detected at peak potential of -0.58 V and −1.85 V with onset potential at about −0.22 and −1.35 V, respectively. During anodic sweep of potentials, another strong emission ECL-3 was obtained at peak potential of 1.20 V with an onset potential of ∼0.90 V. The results revealed that three completely potential-resolved ECL emissions could be obtained using K2S2O8 as a coreactant under a cyclic voltammetry. Meanwhile, the ECL behaviors of GQDs were also investigated using K2S2O8 as a coreactant. ECL-potential curve of GQDs (Fig. 2A, black curve) only showed an ECL peak centered at −1.83 V, which was closed to the peak potential of ECL-2 of ABEI@GQDs, giving rise to the hypothesis that ECL-2 of ABEI@GQDs was assigned to GQDs moiety of ABEI@GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2A (green curve), in comparison with ABEI@GQDs, the mixed solution of ABEI and GQDs with similar concentrations also showed three ECL peaks corresponding to the ECL-1, ECL-2 and ECL-3 of ABEI@GQDs, respectively. The intensities of ECL-1 and ECL-2 for the mixed solution of ABEI and GQDs were similar to that of ABEI@GQDs, while the intensity of ECL-3 for the mixed solution of ABEI and GQDs was about one-fifth of that of ABEI@GQDs. The results indicated that ECL-3 related to ABEI could be enhanced by the formation of the covalent bond between ABEI with GQDs, which might be due to that the surplus −COO– group on the surface of ABEI@GQDs could facilitate radical generation and enhanced the ECL of ABEI, according to the previous study [25]. As a result, a self-enhanced ECL nanoluminophore ABEI@GQDs with three completely potential-resolved ECL emissions were obtained in this work.

To further clarify the three ECL peaks, related cyclic voltammogram (CV) curves were analyzed in the potential ranges from 0 to −2.3 to 1.5 to 0 V. First, the CV curves of ABEI@GQDs in the absence and presence of K2S2O8 were analyzed during cathodic sweep of potentials. In the absence of K2S2O8, a CV peak appeared at −0.73 V (the enlarged CV curve in Figure S2), which also could be observed in the CV curve of blank FTO electrode at −0.86 V. It should be involved the reduction of the dissolved O2 to perhydroxyl anion (HOO−) according to the literature [26]. Moreover, this peak was closed to the above-mentioned ECL-1 peak of ABEI@GQDs, indicating that ECL-1 of ABEI@GQDs was related to the formation of HOO−. The presence of coreactant K2S2O8 resulted in a broad electrochemically irreversible cathodic peak at peak potential of −1.45 V in the CV of ABEI@GQDs (Fig. 2B, red curve), which did not exist in that of ABEI@GQDs without K2S2O8, corresponding to the electro-reduction of S2O8 2− to SO4
− radicals [27]. It is noteworthy that ECL-2 of ABEI@GQDs was in the potential range of this CV peak, which revealed that ECL-2 might be related to the formation of SO4
− radicals. Second, the CV of ABEI@GQDs were analyzed during the anodic sweep of potentials. As shown in the enlarged CV in Fig. 2B, ABEI@GQDs (red curve) demonstrated a small peak at 0.81 V, which was absent in CV of GQDs (black curve) under identical experimental conditions. The results indicated that this CV peak came from the oxidation of ABEI− to ABEI radical (ABEI
−). Moreover, the CV peak was closed to the above-mentioned ECL-3 and thus ECL-3 of ABEI@GQDs might be assigned to the ECL of ABEI.

To more investigate the ECL properties of the proposed multicolor system over wide ranges of both applied potential and emission wavelengths, a series of ECL spectra and images were captured using a CCD photo-detection module and a digital DSLR camera during the 0.05 V/s scans and illustrated as a 3D ECL spectrum shown in Fig. 2C and inset of Figure S3. As it is shown, 3D ECL spectrum and ECL photographs showed that two blue color ECL emissions centered at 452 nm and an orange red color ECL emission centered at 609 nm could be obtained at different potential ranges. In the negative potential range, blue color ECL emission (ECL-1) was obtained at an onset potential of -0.20 V with peak potential at -0.60 V (Figure S4A and S4B) and orange red color ECL emission (ECL-2) was obtained at an onset potential of -1.20 V with peak potential at -1.60 V (Figure S4C and S4D), corresponding to ECL-1 and ECL-2 in the ECL curve of ABEI@GQDs (Fig. 2A, red curve), respectively. In the positive potential range, a blue color ECL emission (ECL-3) could be observed at an onset potential of 0.80 V with peak potential at 1.20 V (Figure S4E and S4F), corresponding to ECL-3 in the ECL curve of ABEI@GQDs (Fig. 2A, red curve).

Moreover, as shown in Figure S3A, S3B and S3C, the ECL spectra of ECL-1, ECL-3 and ECL-2 were individually compared with that of pure ABEI and GQDs under the same conditions. These results demonstrated that ECL-1 and ECL-3 at 452 nm were from ABEI of ABEI@GQDs and ECL-2 at 609 nm was attributed to GQDs moiety of ABEI@GQDs. Moreover, a comparison of fluorescence (FL) spectra and ECL spectra is illustrated in Figure S3D. As it is shown, the emission spectra of ECL-1 and ECL-3 at 452 nm were at the similar wavelength as the FL emission spectrum of ABEI at 433 nm, which further confirmed that ECL-1 and ECL-3 were due to the ABEI moiety of ABEI@GQDs nanoluminophores. Meanwhile, the spectrum of ECL-2 at 609 nm exhibited a red shift (∼60 nm) compared with the FL emission spectrum of GQDs at 545 nm, which might be due to that the ECL emission of quantum dot is more sensitive to the surface chemistry while the FL emissions are susceptibility to the interior [28]. The above comparison further revealed that emission of ECL-2 is from GQDs moiety of ABEI@GQDs nanoluminophores.

To further explain the ECL mechanism of ABEI@GQDs nanoluminophores, various factors influencing the ECL behaviors of ABEI@GQDs, such as N2, O2, K2S2O8 concentration and free radical scavenging experiments, were studied systematically (seeing in SI, section S2.4, S2.5 and S2.6). The results show that ECL-1 and ECL-2 were related to the K2S2O8, and O2 was involved in the reaction of ECL-1 and ECL-3. Further, the free radical scavenging experiments showed that HO
and O2
− took part in ECL-1 reaction, SO4
− participated in ECL-2 reaction and O2
− was involved in ECL-3 reaction. Based on the obtained results and the discussions in the previous section, the possible ECL mechanisms have been proposed for the ABEI@GQDs/S2O8 2− system as shown in Fig. 3 .

The ECL-1 was initially triggered by the electro-reduction of the dissolved oxygen to perhydroxyl anion species i.e. HOO− during the cathodic sweep at lower negative potentials. HOO− was rapidly decomposed to HO
[26]. Subsequently, HO
reacted with O2 and ABEI– to form O2
– and ABEI
–, respectively [29]. Finally, O2
– further reacted with ABEI
– to form the excited state (ABEI-ox*), resulting in the ECL-1 emission. It is noteworthy that the intensity of ECL-1 also increased with increasing the concentration of S2O8 2− (shown in Figure S6) which is most likely due to the synergistic interactions between K2S2O8 and HOO− according to previously report [30].

Subsequently, scanning the potentials towards more negative values initialized electro-reduction of S2O8 2− and GQDs to SO4
− and GQDs radicals (GQDs
−), respectively. The electro-generated radicals underwent series of electron-transfer reactions to generate the excited state of GQDs (GQDs*), resulting in the orange-red ECL-2 emission.

ECL-3 resulted from the oxidation of ABEI– to ABEI
–, and O2 was involved in ECL-3 reactions. ABEI
– could react with O2
– formed by the oxidation of O2 to produce excited-state ABEI-ox*, leading to blue ECL-3 emission [27]. Furthermore, the carboxyl-rich surface of GQDs may also participate in the reaction with O2
– to form peroxycarbonate radicals (−CO4 2
−), which via the reaction with ABEI
– could potentially facilitate the formation of ABEI-ox*, promoting the ECL-3 intensity [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly strategy for label-free ratiometric ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1 , a label-free ratiometric ECL immunosensor was constructed through layer-by-layer assembly. Firstly, a thin film of chitosan was coated on the surface of FTO to form a polymeric membrane with copious amount of free amine groups at the surface. ABEI@GQDs were further bound to the chitosan modified FTO electrode by forming amide bonds between the −COOH group of ABEI@GQDs and the -NH2 group of the chitosan film. In the following step, the anti-cTnI via its -NH2 terminals was further attached to the modified FTO electrode via amide coupling reactions. The nonspecific active binding sites on the prepared modified FTO electrode were subsequently blocked using BSA to form the final BSA-anti-cTnI-ABEI@GQDs-FTO immunosensor.

To monitor the assembly processes of the label-free ratiometric ECL immunosensor, the fabricated procedures were characterized via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and ECL experiments as shown in Figure S8. The details of characterization about immunosensors assembly process were described in SI, section S2.7. The results demonstrated that the ECL immunosensor was assembled successfully. Subsequently combining with cTnI, the intensity of ECL was detected under cyclic voltammetry. Three potential-resolved ECL peaks were obtained and their intensities varied with the concentrations of cTnI. It was found that the ratio of the three ECL peak intensities could be used for the determination of cTnI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the label-free three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensor at different concentrations of cTnI was thoroughly assessed by measuring the ratio I 2 /I 1 /I 3, where I 1 , I 2, and I 3 were denoted as the intensity of ECL-1, ECL-2, and ECL-3, respectively. Optimal experimental conditions of 0.01 M K2S2O8, pH = 11.0, (shown in Figure S9) were considered throughout all the experiments. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with increasing the cTnI concentration, the intensity of ECL-1 and ECL-3 changed slightly and synchronously and the intensity of ECL-2 increased. Moreover, it was found that I 1 /I 3 almost unchanged with increasing the concentration of cTnI (shown in Figure S10). This may be due to both of ECL-1 and ECL-3 from ECL of ABEI. Accordingly, the ratio I 2 /I 1 /I 3 was explored for the quantitative analysis of cTnI, which might further release systematic errors by using I 1 /I 3 as a self-correction reference. As shown in Fig. 4B, I 2 /I 1 /I 3 linearly correlated with the logarithm of cTnI concentration in the range of 1.0 fg/mL to 5.0 pg/mL. The regression equation was I 2 /I 1 /I 3 = 462.6 + 27.9 × log c (unit of c is g/mL) with correlation coefficient of R = 0.9907. The limit of detection was 0.35 fg/mL at the signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The RSD of 5.68% in one day (intra-day precision, n = 6) and 7.21% in six days (inter-day precision, n = 6) for six replicate detections of cTnI at 0.1 pg/mL indicated good stability and reproducibility in the proposed immunosensors. As a comparison, two potential ratio I 2 /I 1 and I2 /I 3 was also used for the quantitative analysis of cTnI as shown in Figure S11. The results using two potential ratio I 2 /I 1 and I 2 /I 3 demonstrated much lower correlation coefficient than that using three potential ratio I 2 /I 1 /I 3, revealing that three potential ratiometry could greatly improve analytical precision. Table S1 shows a comparison between the developed label-free ratiometric ECL immunosensor and other previously reported ECL bioassays considering their sensitivity and analytical performance towards cTnI as the target analyte. The results revealed that the developed ECL immunosensor had a better sensitivity than other ECL methods. Moreover, the developed three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensor is based on three completely potential-resolved ECL peaks, which can effectively eliminate systematic error caused by environmental changes and thus offers more precise detection results than the ECL immonosensors based on two potential ratiometric strategy.

The selectivity of the immunosensor based on antigen-antibody recognition strategy was assessed in a solution of different interfering reagents including Cardiac myoglobin (Mb), heart-type fatty acid binding protein (hFABP), Bovine serum albumins (BSA,) Human serum albumin (HAS) and Human immunoglobulin G (hIgG). The concentration of the interfering reagents was considered to be 10 times higher than that of cTnI. The results in the inset of Fig. 4B clearly illustrated that the proposed immunosensor showed much higher I 2 /I 1 /I 3 values towards cTnI compared to the other interfering participants. The mixed solution of hFABP, Mb, hIgG, BSA, HSA and cTnI, also showed I 2 /I 1 /I 3 values quite similar to the values obtained from cTnI, indicating anti-interference capabilities and high specificity of the proposed immunosensor towards the cTnI analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cTnI in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate potential clinical utility of the proposed label-free three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensor, cTnI levels in human serum samples were analyzed. Human serum samples were collected by the First Affiliated Hospital of Nanjing Medical University and stored at −80 °C. All samples were diluted 100 times by 0.01 M PBS buffer before used for detecting cTnI. The recoveries were in the range of 91–109% (shown in Table S2), indicating that the ratiometric ECL immunosensor possessed a good accuracy for determining cTnI of human serum samples. Moreover, the accuracy for the proposed ratiometric ECL immunosensor was also assessed by comparing the results of the chemiluminescent immunoassay kit (CLIAK). As shown in Table 1 , the results of two detection methods were comparable with RSD ≤ 7.04%. And the proposed immunosensor is label-free, which is faster and simpler than the CLIAK based on labeling technology. Therefore, the label free ratiometric ECL immunosensor could be applied to determine cTnI for AMI patients accurately and rapidly.

A novel potential-resolved dual-color ECL nanoluminophore ABEI@GQDs has been successfully synthesized, which has demonstrated three completely potential-resolved ECL peaks in the presence of coreactant K2S2O8 solution. In the negative potential range, ECL-1 with peak potential of -0.58 V and maximal emission wavelength of 452 nm and ECL-2 with peak potential of -1.85 V and maximal emission wavelength of 609 nm were obtained. In the positive potential range, ECL-3 with peak potential of 1.20 V and maximal emission wavelength of 452 nm was observed. It was suggested that ECL-1 was due to the reaction of OH
decomposed by electro-reduced HOO− with ABEI− and O2. ECL-2 was due to that GQDs
− from electro-reduced GQDs reacted with SO4
− electro-reduced from S2O8 2−. ECL-3 was due to that ABEI
– electro-oxidized from ABEI− in positive potential reacted with O2
– formed by the oxidation of O2. On this basis, a label-free three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensor was established for the determination of AMI biomarker cTnI with a linear range from 1.0 fg/mL to 5.0 pg/mL and a detection limit of 0.35 fg/mL. The sensitivity is superior to previously reported ECL methods for the determination of cTnI. And the immunosensor can effectively eliminate errors caused by environmental changes and thus offers more precise determination results than two potential ratiometric ECL immunosensors, whereas potential-resolved differential electrochemiluminescence immunosensor based on previously developed MOF-5-wrapped CdS quantum dot can effectively release the additive systematical error. This work also provides new way to develop new nanoluminophores with multiple potential-resolved ECL emissions for three potential ratiometric ECL immunosensors by using one ECL molecule with two potential-resolved ECL emissions as a self-correction reference, effectively releasing systematic errors. Additionally, although the proposed immunosensor is the demonstration of proof-of-concept and it is difficult to use it for the determination of high-throughput samples, printed electrode arrays combined with the proposed strategy for high-throughput samples are under further investigation.

Mingquan Guo: Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Jiangnan Shu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration. Dexin Du: Data curation, Investigation. Mohammad A. Haghighatbin: Data curation, Investigation. Di Yang: Resources. Zhiping Bian: Resources. Hua Cui: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The support of this research by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFA0201300), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21804124, 21874122 and 21527807), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2019M662154), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. WK2060190097) and the Chinese Academy of Sciences President’s International Fellowship Initiative (Grant No. 2020PB0062) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129628.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mingquan Guo received his BS degree in Chemistry from Anhui Normal University in 2014, and now is a Ph.D. student in the University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, China. His research interest is electrochemiluminescence biosensors for in vitro diagnosis.

Jiangnan Shu received his Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry from the University of Science and Technology of China in 2018, and now is a postdoctor at the University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, China. His research interests focus on the synthesis of novel electrochemiluminescence nanomaterials and their applications in in vitro diagnosis.

Dexin Du received his BS degree in Applied Chemistry from Shanxi University in 2016, and now is a Ph.D. student in the University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, China. His research interest is electrochemiluminescence biosensors for in vitro diagnosis.

Mohammad A. Haghighatbin received his Ph.D. in 2017 from La Trobe University, Melbourne, Australia. He is currently holding the Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) President’s International Fellowship Initiative (PIFI) postdoctoral fellowship at the University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, China. His research interests include electrochemiluminescence of novel inorganic and organic materials and their applications in miniaturized electroanalytical devices.

Di Yang is a professor of Clinical Medicine at Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, China. His research interests include pathogenesis of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, vascular endothelium and cardiovascular disease, and research and application of cardiac markers.
Zhiping Bian received her MS degree in 2004 from Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, China. Now, she is engaged in Nanjing Medical University and works on antibody preparation and purification.

Hua Cui is a professor at the University of Science and Technology of China (USTC), Hefei, China. She received his Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry in 1990 from USTC. Her research interests include novel chemiluminescence and electrochemiluminescence functionalized nanomaterials and their applications in in vitro diagnosis.
